draft.introduction: Aeneid Book 2 contains Aeneas’s narration of the fall of Troy, including the Trojan Horse, the death of Priam, and the escape with his father Anchises on his shoulders. It is the most influential account of a city’s destruction in Western literature.

translation.text: All fell silent and kept their eyes fixed intently upon him. Then father Aeneas began from his high couch: ‘Unspeakable, O Queen, is the grief you bid me renew. But if your desire to learn of our disaster is so great, though my mind shudders to remember and has recoiled in pain, I will begin.’ And of the Horse: ‘I fear Greeks even bearing gifts.’
translation.original_text: Conticuere omnes intentique ora tenebant; inde toro pater Aeneas sic orsus ab alto: Infandum, regina, iubes renovare dolorem

translator_notes.note: Laocoon’s warning timeo Danaos et dona ferentes (“I fear Greeks even bearing gifts”) became one of the most quoted Latin phrases in history. The line’s irony is that the warning goes unheeded—a pattern Virgil uses to explore the tragic gap between knowledge and action.
